paragraph-intro: "My work starts with the body — specifically, with everything the body is told to hide. Raised in Canada by my Uruguayan parents, I learned early that culture shapes how we express and relate to our bodies, and that unlearning those social limits offers the freedom to choose our own. Throughout my career, I've been making space for sensuality, queerness, and intimacy to be examined honestly and without shame. My work has taken many forms: internationally touring performances and queer films, a thriving somatic sexology practice, and a ballroom community I built from the ground up in Montréal."
paragraph-chouinard:
GERARD X REYES is a queer, multidisciplinary Uruguayan-Canadian artist (they/he) based in Montréal/Tiohtià:ke. As a choreographer, dancer, filmmaker, Certified Somatic Sexologist, and intimacy coordinator, their work moves across performance, installation, film, and somatic education — always at the intersection of queerness, sensuality, and the body as a site of knowledge.

Following seven years as a company member of the internationally acclaimed Compagnie Marie Chouinard (2006–2013) — during which they performed eight repertory works across Europe, Asia, and the Americas and received a Gemini Award for Best Performance in Chouinard's film bODY_rEMIX — Reyes returned to New York to train with voguing legends including Leiomy Maldonado, Jose Xtravaganza, Benny Ninja, and Godfather Archie Burnett Ninja. This research culminated in their critically acclaimed debut solo The Principle of Pleasure (2015), which played to sold-out audiences at Festival TransAmériques (Montréal), SummerWorks (Toronto), Tanzfabrik (Berlin), and Sziget Music Festival (Budapest), among others, touring internationally from 2015 to 2023.
paragraph-pop:
Their multimedia series Public / Private Parts (2022–2025) explores a spectrum of queer intimacy and raw embodiment in an era where eroticism is commodified, mediated, and flattened to the 2D screen. Developed in collaboration with queer sex workers and somatic educators in Berlin, the 5-part series encompasses a live performance-video installation (Festival TransAmériques, Usine C, Parcours Danse), two short films (ECSTATIC UNITY; The Key to Dreams), a documentary interview collection, and a podcast tracing the intersections of moral censorship, pornography, and erotic education. The research phase also led Reyes to complete their certification as a Somatic Sexologist. Their debut short films have toured to 37 festivals internationally — Chéries-Chéris (Paris), Fringe! Queer Film and Arts (London), BLUSH (San Francisco), Xposed (Berlin), FID:RIO (Rio de Janeiro), and beyond.

In 2023, Reyes was commissioned as the choreographer and movement coach for rising Québécoise filmmaker Sophie Dupuis' feature film SOLO, which received the Best Canadian Feature Film award at TIFF. That same year, they appeared as a guest performer in acclaimed theatre director Philip Cyr's production of Martin Crimp's Atteintes à sa vie.
paragraph-ballroom:
Between 2013 and 2019, Reyes was a founding force in Montréal's Kiki Ballroom scene — teaching voguing classes, mentoring emerging 2SLGBTQ+ artists, and producing small- and large-scale balls for Fierté Montréal and Pride Canada. Committed to connecting the community with its roots, Reyes brought New York City icons including Mike Q, Precious, Twiggy Pucci Garçon, and Chi Chi Mizrahi to the city for the first time.

Reyes' most recent journeys have taken them beyond the city and the stage. Guided by conservationist Jak Maliszewski, they spent time in sanctuaries and various ecosystems across Peru, Namibia, Belize, and Australia, caring for wild animals, adapting to extreme environments, and living and learning alongside communities whose kinship to land, animals, and one another has not been severed.
paragraph-bodywork: Their new performance work The Inverted Kingdom is rooted in this research. Inspired by Uruguayan artist Joaquín Torres-García's painting Inverted America, Reyes is excavating real stories from their paternal family tree, as well as the hidden queer and Afro-Uruguayan roots of tango by studying it as a social and listening practice. Creation residencies are planned in Montevideo in 2026–27 in collaboration with Uruguayan tango artist Rodrigo Fleitas and institutional partners including the Museo Nacional de Artes Visuales (MNAV), El Instituto Nacional de Artes Escénicas (INAE), and Espacio de Arte Contemporáneo (EAC).
